content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etiam_p99_l2"> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">et jam</foreign>), still, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eludet_p99_l2"> , <gloss>
							baffle</gloss>, i.e. his mad conduct <gloss>makes fools of</gloss> the
							Roman people, as it were, by continuing to escape the just punishment
							that would suppress it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quem_finem_p99_l2">:
							almost equivalent to <foreign xml:lang="lat">quamdiu</foreign>, but
							implying some shock or crisis (<foreign xml:lang="lat">finem</foreign>)
							which must follow. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sese_jactabit_p99_l3">
							, <gloss>insolently display itself</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nihil_p99_l3">
							(adv. acc.), <gloss>not at all</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Palati_p99_l4">:
							one of the strongest positions in the city, commanding the Forum, and so
							most likely to be seized by the conspirators. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">Palatium</foreign>, an isolated hill, of a rudely
							quadrangular shape, was the original seat of the city of Rome, from
							which the city spread gradually over the other hills. In the last years
							of the Republic, the Palatine became the fashionable place for
							residences. Here was Cicero's house as well as Catiline's. It was
							because of its nearness to his house, as well as because of the strength
							of its position, that Cicero selected this temple for the meeting of the
							Senate on this occasion. Under the Empire the Palatine became the seat
							of the imperial residence, and its name, <gloss>
								palace</gloss>, has passed in this sense into most modern languages. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bonorum_p99_l5">:
							the Senate was surrounded by a crowd of <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								equites</foreign> and other citizens (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.21">21</ref>, below). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="locus_p100_l1">:
							the Senate was assembled, not, as usual, in the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Curia
								Hostilia</foreign>, but in the Temple of Jupiter Stator, which
							occupied a commanding position on the brow of the Palatine Hill and
							faced the Sacred Way. The ruins of this temple were discovered some
							years ago (see view in text). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="horum_p100_l1">
							(with a gesture), i.e. the Senators present. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ora_p100_l1"> , <gloss>
							features</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voltus_p100_l1"> , <gloss>
							expression</gloss>: the phrase is a sort of hendiadys, almost equivalent
							to <gloss>expression of their features</gloss>; § <ref n="AG 640">640</ref> (385); B. 374, 4; G. 698; H. 751,3, N.1 (636,
							iii, 2); H-B. 631,5. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patere_p100_l2">:
							note the emphatic position. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_p100_l2">:
							observe the abruptness and force given by omitting the interrog.
							particle -<foreign xml:lang="lat">ne</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="constrictam_teneri_p100_l2">
							, <gloss>is held fast bound</gloss>; § <ref n="AG 497">497</ref>, b
							(292, c) ; cf. B. 337, 6; G. 238; H.431,1(385, 1, N.); H.-B. 605,5. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="proxima_p100_l4">
							, <foreign xml:lang="lat">superiore</foreign>: for what was done on the
							night of Nov. 6, see sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.4">4</ref>; as to <foreign xml:lang="lat">proxima</foreign>, <gloss>last night</gloss>, we meet
							with nothing but general assertions. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tempora_p100_l7">,
							etc., <gloss>what a time! what a state of things!</gloss> (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mores_p100_l7">
								<gloss>customs
							of the time</gloss>.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="immo_p100_l8"> , <gloss>nay
							more</gloss>: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="immo_p100_l8_2">
							here negatives not the <gloss>fact</gloss> of the preceding statement (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vivit_p100_l8">),
							but only its <gloss>form</gloss> as not being strong enough; <gloss>
								nay</gloss> is similarly used in English, as in <gloss>Midsummer-Night's
							Dream</gloss>, iii, 2, 313: "To strike me, spurn me, nay, to kill me
							too." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videmur_p100_l11">,
							etc. <gloss>think we do enough for</gloss> (i.e. fulfil our duty to the
							state). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_vitemus_p100_l11">:
							in the dir. form, <foreign xml:lang="lat">satisfacimus si
								vitamus</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_mortem_p100_l12">:
							the consuls originally possessed full powers of judgment in criminal
							cases, including punishment by death. These highest powers of the <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign> were suspended within the city by
							laws which gave the right of appeal to the people (note, <ref n="pl110.16">p. 110, l. 16</ref>), but the Senate could revive them
							in cases of danger by the formula <foreign xml:lang="lat">Videant
								consules ne quid res publica detrimenti capiat</foreign>, — a
							proceeding analogous to the proclamation of martial law. This action the
							Senate had taken Oct.21, nearly three weeks before. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportebat_p100_l13">,
							apod. of an implied cond.: § <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (311, c); B.
							304, 3, a; G. 254, R1; H. 583 (511, I, N.1); H-B. 582, 3, a; the imperf.
							is used with <foreign xml:lang="lat">jam
								pridem</foreign>, where in English we might expect the pluperf.; § <ref n="AG 471">471</ref>, b (277, b); B. 260,4; G. 234; H. 535(469,2);
							H.-B. 485; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportebat_p100_l13_2">
							alone would mean "you ought [now] to be [but are not]"; with <foreign xml:lang="lat">jam pridem</foreign> it means "you ought to have been
							long ago and still ought to be." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_diu_p100_l14">:
							words in brackets are thought to be spurious insertions in the text. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="an_vero_p100_l14">
							properly belongs both to <foreign xml:lang="lat">interfecit</foreign>
							and <foreign xml:lang="lat">perferemus</foreign>; in English we should
							connect the two clauses by <gloss>and</gloss>. On the force of <foreign xml:lang="lat">an</foreign>, see § <ref n="AG 335">335</ref>, b (211, b); B. 162, 4, a; G. 457, 1 ; H. 380,
							3 (353, N.4); H.-B. 236. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vir_amplissimus_p100_l14">
							, <foreign xml:lang="lat">pontifex maximus</foreign>: observe how these
							words strengthen the force of the example. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ti_Gracchium_p100_l15">:
							Tiberius Sempronius Gracchus, a young man of high rank and great purity
							of character, attempted to carry through some important reforms,
							particularly touching the tenure of the public lands, <date when="-0133">B.C.
								133</date>. Requiring more time to make his legislation effective,
							he attempted illegally to secure his own re-election as tribune, when he
							was attacked and killed by a mob of Senators headed by P. Scipio Nasica. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="privatus_p100_l16">:
							at the time referred to, Nasica was only a private citizen of consular
							rank. He afterwards went into exile, and was made Pontifex Maximus in
							his absence. The word <foreign xml:lang="lat">privatus</foreign> is rhetorically opposed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nos_consules_p100_l16">
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_p100_l18"> , <gloss>that
							case</gloss>, plural for singular as referring to the circumstances of
							the case. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ahala_p100_l19">:
							the <foreign xml:lang="lat">magister equitum</foreign> of the famous
							Cincinnatus; he killed without legal process the <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								eques</foreign> Maelius, on suspicion that the latter was aiming at
							royal power (<date when="-0439">B.C. 439</date>); </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="novis_rebus_p100_l19">
							(the classic expression for a violent change of government), <gloss>
							revolution</gloss>: dat. after <foreign xml:lang="lat">studentem</foreign>
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuit_p100_l20">
							(emphat.), <gloss>there was</gloss>, etc., implying that it is so no
							longer; § <ref n="AG 598">598</ref>, d (344 d, 3). Cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">fuit Ilium</foreign>, <ref n="Verg. A. 2.325">Aeneid
								2.325</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="habemus_p100_l22">
							(emphat.), i.e. <gloss>it is not that we lack</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senatus_consultum_p100_l22">:
							i.e. the decree conferring dictatorial power on the consuls (see note on
							sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.2">2</ref>, 1.12, above), <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut videant consules</foreign>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vehemens_p100_l23">
							, <gloss>severe</gloss>, as regards Catiline; grave, <gloss>carrying
							weight</gloss>, and so justifying the consuls in any extreme measures. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_deest_p100_l23">,
							etc., <gloss>it is not that the state lacks wise counsels</gloss>, etc.,
							but that the consuls are remiss in executing them. <milestone n="2" unit="chapter"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="decrevit_p100_l26">:
							translate, to preserve the emphasis, <gloss>there was once a
								decree</gloss>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_videret_p100_l26">,
							subst. clause of purp., obj. of <foreign xml:lang="lat">decrevit</foreign>:
							§ <ref n="AG 563">563</ref> (331); B. 295, 4; G. 546; H. 564, i (498);
							H-B. 502, 3, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Opimius_p100_l26">:
							Lucius Opimius was consul <date when="-0121">B.C. 121</date>, when Caius
							Gracchus, the younger brother of Tiberius, was attempting to carry
							through a series of measures far more revolutionary than those of his
							brother. The Senate took alarm, and entrusted the consul with absolute
							power. In the tumult that ensued, some 3,000 are said to have lost their
							lives, including Gracchus and his leading associate, Fulvius. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_caperet_p100_l27">,
							obj. of <foreign xml:lang="lat">videret</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="interfectus_est_p100_l28">
							(emphat.), i.e. in that case death was promptly inflicted. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patre_p100_l29">:
							Tiberius Gracchus, the elder, one of the most eminent statesmen of his
							day. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="avo_p100_l29">:
							Scipio Africanus, the conqueror of Hannibal. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Mario_p101_l1">
							(dat. after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="permissa_p101_l1">):
							this was in Marius' sixth consulship (<date when="-0100">B.C.
								100</date>). He was secretly in league with the revolutionists,
							—Saturninus and Servilius Glaucia, corrupt demagogues, unworthy
							imitators of the noble Gracchi. When it came to the point, however, the
							courage of Marius failed him: he deserted his accomplices, and joined
							the Senate in crushing the revolt. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rei_publicae_p101_l3">:
							poss. gen., the punishment being looked on as something belonging to the
							party avenged, and exacted from the other party as a payment due. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="remorata_est_p101_l4">
							(governing Saturninum, etc.), <gloss>keep Saturninus and
								Servilius
								waiting</gloss>, i.e. did they have to wait one day, etc.? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vicesimum_p101_l4">:
							strictly speaking, it was now (Nov. 6) the 19th day by Roman reckoning
							from Oct. 21; cf. § <ref n="AG 424">424</ref>, c (259, c); G. 336, R.1 </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patimur_p101_l4">:
							for tense, see § <ref n="AG 466">466</ref> (276, a); B. 259, 4; G.
							230; H. 532, 2 (467, 2); H.-B. 485. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="horum_p101_l5">,
							i.e. the Senate. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujusce_modi_p101_l6">,
							i.e. like those just mentioned; § <ref n="AG 146">146</ref>, a, N.1
							(101, footnote); B. 87, footnote 2; G. 104, I, N.1; H. 178, 3 (186, I);
							H.-B. 138, 2, c. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tabulis_p101_l6">:
							brazen tablets, on which the laws, etc., were inscribed. The edict is
							said to be <gloss>shut up</gloss> in them (until put in force), <gloss>like
							a sword hidden in its scabbard</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="interfectum_esse_p101_l8">:
							§ <ref n="AG 486">486</ref>, b and N. (288, d); B. 270, 2, a; G. 280,
							2; H-B. 582, 31 a, footnote 2 </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="convenit_p101_l8">,
							perf.: § <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (311, c); B. 304,3, a; G. 254,
							a.1; H. 583(511, I, N.1); H-B. 582,3, a. <pb n="365"/>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ad_deponendam_p101_l9">,
							etc.: § <ref n="AG 506">506</ref> (300); B. 338, 3; G. 432; H. 628,
							623 (542, iii, 544, I); H.-B. 384, 3, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cupio_p101_l10">
							(emphat.), <gloss>I am anxious</gloss>: a concession, opposed by <foreign xml:lang="lat">sed</foreign>, below. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_esse_p101_l10">:
							§ <ref n="AG 563">563</ref>, b, I (331, b, N.); B. 331, iv, a; G. 532,
							R.2; H. 614 (535, ii); H.-B. 586, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dissolutum_p101_l11">
							, <gloss>arbitrary</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ipse_p101_l12">:
							Latin in such cases emphasizes the subject; English, the object; § <ref n="AG 298">298</ref>,f(195, 1); B. 249, 2; G. 311, 2; H. 509, I
							(452, I); H-B. 268. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inertiae_p101_l12">:
							§ <ref n="AG 352">352</ref> (220); B. 228, 2; G. 378; H. 456 (409, U);
							H.-B. 342. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="castra_sunt_p101_l13">,
							etc.: an enumeration of the circumstances which make a mild policy no
							longer possible. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="faucibus_p101_l14">
							, <gloss>narrow pass</gloss>, leading north from Etruria, through the
							Apennines. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conlocata_p101_l14">:
							§ <ref n="AG 495">495</ref> (291, b); B. 337, 2; G. 250, a.2; H. 538,
							4 (47 I, 6, N.1); H.-B. 320, iii, 248. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_p101_l18"> , <gloss>at
							once</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erit_verendum_p101_l19">,
							etc., <gloss>I shall have to fear, I suppose</gloss> (ironical), <gloss>that
							all good citizens will fail to say</gloss> (lit. <gloss>will not
								say</gloss> ) <gloss>that I have acted too late rather than that
							anybody will say
								that I have acted too cruelly</gloss>, i.e. I shall have to fear
							that I shall be accused of cruelty rather than slackness. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_dicat_p101_l19">:
							§ <ref n="AG 564">564</ref> (331,f); B. 296, 2, a; G. 550, 2; H. 567,
							2 (498, iii, N.2); H.-B. 502, 4. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="boni_p101_l19">
							(sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">dicant</foreign>): here, as usual, <gloss>the
							well-intentioned</gloss>, i.e. those who held the speaker's views. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_p101_l21">:
							opposed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omnes_boni_p101_l21">
							(1.19, above). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="factum_esse_p101_l21">:
							§ <ref n="AG 486">486</ref>, b and N. (288, d); B. 270, 2, a; G. 280,
							a.2; H.-B. 582, 3, a, footnote 2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportuit_p101_l21">:
							§ <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (311, c) ; B. 304, 3, a; G. 597, a.3,
							a; H. 583 (51 I, I, N.') ; H-B. 582, 3, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="denique_p101_l22">,
							i.e. then, and not before. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_p101_l23"> , <gloss>at
							length</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fateatur_p101_l24">:
							for mood, see § <ref n="AG 537">537</ref>, 2 (319, 2); B. 284, 2; G.
							631, I; H. 589, ii, 591 (509, 1); H.-B. 521, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_vivis_p101_l26">,
							just <gloss>as you are</gloss> [now] <gloss>living</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_possis_p101_l27">:
							purpose (not result). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etiam_p101_l28"> , <gloss>
							besides</gloss> the forces on guard. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="speculabuntur_p101_l29">:
							probably referring to the spies in the interest of the government, who
							were in the very heart of the conspiracy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_amplius_p102_l1">,
							etc., <gloss>what is there for you to wait for more?</gloss>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_exspectes_p102_l1">:
							rel. clause of purpose. <pb n="366"/>
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inlustrantur_p102_l4">,
							opposed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obscurare_p102_l4">
							; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erumpunt_p102_l4">,
							to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="continere_p102_l4">
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conflatam_p109_l19"> , <gloss>run
						together</gloss> (like molten metal). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_p109_l19">, i.e. in
						this band. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bacchabere_p109_l21"> , <gloss>will
						revel</gloss>. To a Roman the word suggested the wild orgies of the frenzied
						Bacchanals, so that it is much stronger than our <gloss>
							revel</gloss>, which in course of time has become rather vague: cf.
						Aeneid 4.301 (and illustrations). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="meditati_sunt_p109_l23"> , <gloss>have
						been practised</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="feruntur_p109_l23"> , <gloss>are
						talked about</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="labores_p109_l23">: cf. <ref>Sallust
						Catiline 5</ref>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">L.
							Catilina
							nobili genere natus fuit, magna vi et animi et corporis, sed
							ingenio
							malo pravoque. Huic ab adulescentia bella intestina caedes
							rapinae
							discordia civilis grata fuere ibique juventutem suam
							exercuit. Corpus
							patiens inediae algoris vigiliae supra quam cuiquam
							credibile est</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="facinus_p109_l25"> , <gloss>deed
						of violence</gloss>, contrasted with <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							stuprum</foreign> , <gloss>debauchery</gloss>; just as <foreign xml:lang="lat">bonis
							otiosorum</foreign>, <gloss>property of peaceful citizens</gloss>, is
						with <foreign xml:lang="lat">somno maritorum</foreign>, <gloss>the
							repose of husbands</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ubi_ostentes_p109_l26">
						(purpose clause), <gloss>opportunity to display</gloss> (lit. a place, <gloss>
						where</gloss>, etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reppuli_p109_l29">: § <ref n="AG 545">545</ref>(323, I) B. 288, I, a; G. 580; H. 600 (5 1, i);
						H.-B. 550 and a. Cicero here takes credit to himself for using his influence
						as consul to defeat the election of Catiline. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exsul_p109_l30"> , </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consul_p109_l30">: observe
						the play upon words (see Vocab.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="latrocinium_p109_l32">: cf.
						note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">latrocinio</foreign>, l. 1, above. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="querimoniam_p110_l2">,
						i.e. for not having suppressed the conspiracy more vigorously. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="detester_deprecer_p110_l2">
						(construed with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="a_me_p110_l2">, above), <gloss>remove
						by protest and plea</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patria_p110_l4">: the
						personified <foreign xml:lang="lat">patria</foreign> is dramatically
						introduced as accusing Cicero of remissness in letting Catiline go unharmed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="M_Tulli_p110_l6"> (voc.):
						the regular way of formal address; the use of the family name (<foreign xml:lang="lat">Cicero</foreign>) is more familiar. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="evocatorem_servorum_p110_l9">
						, <gloss>a summoner of slaves</gloss>, i.e. to enlist under him against the
						state. To the Romans (as to all peoples who, having a large slave
						population, are in constant fear of servile revolts) such an accusation was
						the most violent reproach conceivable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="duci_mactari_p110_l12">;
						§ <ref n="AG 563">563</ref>, a, N. (311, a, N.1); Cf. B. 295, 4, N.; G.
						546, N.3; H. 614 (535, ii); H-B. 587, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rogatae_sunt_p110_l16">:
						the magistrate who proposed a law formally asked the people whether they
						would accept it; hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">rogo</foreign> was the word
						regularly used for this act, and the proposition itself was called <foreign xml:lang="lat">rogatio</foreign>. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">leges</foreign> in question, <foreign xml:lang="lat">Valeria, Porcia</foreign>, and <foreign xml:lang="lat">Sempronia</foreign> (of Caius Gracchus), were enacted to
						protect — like our laws securing the <foreign xml:lang="lat">habeas
							corpus</foreign> and trial by jury — the life and liberty of citizens
						against the arbitrary power of magistrates, which in this case would
						apparently be used by Cicero. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_numquam_p110_l16">,
						etc.: as a fact, however, the precedents here referred to had been really
						violations of the constitution. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praeclaram_gratiam_p110_l18">
						, <gloss>you show a noble gratitude</gloss> (cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">habere
							gratiam</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">agere gratias</foreign> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nulla_majorum_p110_l20">:
						though by the Roman constitution the higher offices were open to all
						citizens, yet it was rare that a man whose ancestors had not held these
						offices could succeed in attaining them himself. If, like Cicero, he did so,
						he was called a <foreign xml:lang="lat">novus
							homo</foreign>, and his descendants belonged to the nobility. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tam_mature_p110_l20">:
						Cicero attained the quaestorship, the praetorship, and the consulship (<foreign xml:lang="lat">honorum gradiis</foreign>) at the earliest age possible
						in each case. This was a mark of public confidence which had never happened
						to a <foreign xml:lang="lat">novus homo</foreign> before. <pb n="377"/>
					</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="invidiae_p110_l22">, i.e.
						the odium which might attach to the consuls apparently exceeding his
						constitutional authority. In fact Cicero was later brought to trial and
						exiled on this very charge. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="num_est_p110_l24"> , <gloss>pray
						is</gloss> (implying strong negation): § <ref n="AG 332">
							332</ref>, b (210, c); B. 162,2, b; G. 464, R.; H. 378(351, I, N.8);
						H-B. 231, I, (1. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inertiae_p110_l25">, (sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">invidia</foreign>), the <gloss>
							reproach</gloss> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="an_p110_l25"> belongs with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_existimas_p110_l25"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conflagraturum_p110_l27"> , <gloss>will
						be consumed</gloss> (lit. <gloss>will burn up</gloss> ).
									<milestone n="110.29" unit="pg_l"/>
					</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="idem_sentiunt_p110_l27"> , <gloss>have
						the same views</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mentibus_p110_l27"> , <gloss>
						thoughts</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="factu_p110_l30">, the rare
						"latter supine": § <ref n="AG 510">510</ref> (303); B. 340, 2; G. 436; H.
						635 and 4 (547 and N.1); H-B. 619, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gladiatori_p110_l31">: the
						gladiators were trained slaves owned by rich men, and were often employed as
						bullies in political campaigns. Hence the word came almost to mean <gloss>ruffian,
						"bruiser," "thug."</gloss>
					</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_honestarunt_p111_l1">:
						notice that the simple condition here expresses cause; § <ref n="AG 515">515</ref>, a, N. (306, a, N.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="superiorum_p111_l2"> , <gloss>before
						them</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_pervenerit_p111_l2"> , </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fore_p111_l2"> (fut.,
						indir. disc.), ll. 15, 16; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_interfecto_p111_l2"> , </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="posse_p111_l2"> (fut.,
						indir. disc., <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> disguised), ll.
						18-20; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_exstinguetur_p111_l2">
						(fut., more vivid), ll.20-22. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="maxime_p111_l6"> , <gloss>ever
						so much</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_putarem_p111_l7">,
						result clause explaining hoc (not a subst. clause). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="partam_p111_l7"> (from <foreign xml:lang="lat">pario</foreign>), <gloss>acquired</gloss> (a very common
						meaning). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_nulli_p111_l8">, etc.:
						it should be remembered that there were many well-intentioned citizens who
						either doubted the existence of a conspiracy or thought Cicero's fears of it
						greatly exaggerated; and that' even among those who admitted the fact there
						was considerable variety of partisan feeling. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videant_p111_l9"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">dissimulent</foreign>: subj. of charact. (not coord. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dicerent_p111_l9">),
						expressing the <emph>character</emph> of the men referred to, while </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aluerunt_p111_l9">, etc.
						(l. 11), merely gives additional facts about them (hence indic.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="regie_p111_l14"> , <gloss>
						despotically</gloss>: the Roman idea of a king and kingly government was
						associated with Tarquinius Superbus. Here the word also implies the
						assumption of unlawful power (= <foreign xml:lang="lat">tyrannice</foreign>), as well as its abuse. <pb n="378"/>
					</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_p111_l15"> , <gloss>as
						it is</gloss>
					</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quo_p111_l15">: § <ref n="AG 308">308</ref>, g (201,f 2); cf. G. 611, R.1; the antecedent is </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_castra_p111_l15"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="improbum_p111_l17"> , <gloss>
						dishonest</gloss>
					</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_interfecto_p111_l18">,
						disguised fut. <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign>; § <ref n="AG 521">521</ref>, a (310, a); B. 305, I; G. 600, I; H. 575, 9 (507,
						N.7); H-B. 578, 6; the apod. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="posse_p111_l18"> is fut. in
						sense: § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, d, 584, b (307, d); G. 248, R.; H.-B.
						472, d. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejecerit_p111_l20">: for
						tense, see § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, c (307, c); G. 244, 2; H. 574, 2
						(508, 2); Cf. H.-B. 577, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eodem_p111_l21"> , <gloss>to
						the same place</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adulta_p111_l22"> , <gloss>
						full-grown</gloss>, as opposed to <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							stirps</foreign> , <gloss>the root</gloss> (properly the <gloss>
							stock</gloss> from which new shoots may spring out), and semen, the
						seed. <milestone n="13" unit="chapter"/>
					</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_diu_p111_l25">: the
						conspiracy was ready to break out <date when="-0065">B.C. 65</date> (see
						note on <ref n="pl105.15">p. 105, l. 15</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="versamur_p111_l26"> , <gloss>have
						lived</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nescio_pacto_p111_l26">
						, <gloss>somehow or other</gloss>; § <ref n="AG 575">575</ref>, d (344,
						a); B. 253, 6; G. 467, N.; Cf. H. 512, 7 (455, 2); H-B. 537, e. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="veteris_p111_l27"> (sharply
						contrasted with <foreign xml:lang="lat">nostri</foreign>), i.e. the disease
						is of long standing, but its outbreak has occurred just in my consulship. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="visceribus_p111_l32"> , <gloss>
						vitals</gloss> (properly the great interior organs, as the heart, lungs,
						etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aestu_febrique_p112_l1"> , <gloss>the
						heat of fever</gloss> (hendiadys). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reliquis_vivis_p112_l4">:
						abl. absolute. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="circumstare_p112_l8"> , <gloss>hang
						round</gloss>, for the purpose of intimidation: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">praetor urbanus</foreign> had his tribunal in the Forum. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="patefacta_p112_l15"> , <gloss>laid
						bare</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inlustrata_p112_l15"> , <gloss>set
						in full light</gloss>; <foreign xml:lang="lat">
							oppressa</foreign> , <gloss>crushed</gloss>; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vindicata_p112_l15"> , <gloss>
						punished</gloss>. Observe the climax. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ominibus_p113_l1"> , <gloss>
						prospects</gloss>. What Cicero has just said (p.112,11. I 1-16) makes the <foreign xml:lang="lat">omen</foreign> under which Catiline is to depart, — an
						omen of good for the state, but of evil for him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Juppiter_p113_l4">: thus
						the oration closes with a prayer to Jupiter Stator, in whose temple the
						Senate was now assembled. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Statorem_p113_l6"> , <gloss>the
						Stay</gloss>. The name was apparently first given to Jupiter as the <gloss>
						Stayer</gloss> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">
							sto</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sisto</foreign>) of flight (see
						note to sect. 11, p.104, 1. I), but it is here applied to him as the <gloss>
						Stay</gloss> (supporter) of the Roman state, a meaning which the word may
						well have from its derivation. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="arcebis_p113_l8">, used as
						a mild imperative; § <ref n="AG 449">449</ref>, b (269,f); B. 261, 3; G.
						2651; H. 560, 4, N. (487, 4); H.-B. 572. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="latrones_p113_l9">: cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">latrocinium</foreign> in sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.27">27</ref> (<ref n="pl109.32">p. 109, l.
							32</ref> ). </gloss>
